researchFindings.claim: The most important rule of shelter selection is to use what materials are available; knowing multiple shelter types lets you adapt to any environment
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Best Survival Shelter: A-Frame, Debris, Teepee, Lean-To?
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core selection principle — framing for the reference guide

researchFindings.claim: A tarp shelter is the fastest option when you have a tarp; it can be rigged in minutes and provides excellent rain protection but poor insulation in cold weather
researchFindings.sourceTitle: 5 Wilderness Survival Shelters You Should Know How to Build
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Tarp shelter profile — fast but not warm without sleeping gear

researchFindings.claim: A natural lean-to can be built in under an hour with branches alone; it provides good wind and rain protection when properly oriented but poor insulation — requires a fire on the open side for warmth in cold conditions
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Best Survival Shelter: A-Frame, Debris, Teepee, Lean-To?
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Lean-to profile — speed vs. warmth trade-off

researchFindings.claim: A debris hut takes several hours to build but provides the best thermal retention of natural shelter types; it can maintain survivable body temperature down to 0°F without fire in a forested environment
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Debris hut profile — slow to build but most effective in extreme cold

researchFindings.claim: Choose a lean-to when: you have fire capability, weather is mild, you need shelter quickly, or you may need to signal rescuers from the open side
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Decision rule — when lean-to is the right choice

researchFindings.claim: Choose a debris hut when: temperatures are dangerously cold, you cannot start a fire, you are in a forested area with abundant leaf litter, and you expect to overnight without fire support
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Decision rule — when debris hut is the right choice

researchFindings.claim: No shelter type compensates for inadequate ground insulation; sleeping on bare ground causes rapid conductive heat loss regardless of overhead cover type
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Universal warning applicable to all shelter types

researchFindings.claim: Do not build a shelter larger than your body requires; excess internal volume cannot be warmed by body heat alone
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Universal warning — oversizing is the most common mistake in natural shelter construction

researchFindings.claim: A debris hut is not feasible in arid, alpine, or desert environments where leaf litter is scarce; in these environments lean-to or tarp shelter with supplemental insulation (emergency blanket) is the correct choice
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Environment-specific decision — prevents wasted effort in wrong terrain

recommendation: Write a reference_guide that helps users choose between tarp shelter, natural lean-to, and debris hut based on available materials, temperature, fire capability, and time available. Use a decision framework (whenToUse, preferredAction, backupAction) and a clear comparison of each shelter type's speed vs. warmth trade-off. No stepByStepActions for a reference guide. Warnings should focus on universal mistakes (oversizing, no ground insulation) that apply across all types.
